parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin dis-, here suggesting an unfolding or spreading out — not reversing something, but opening it up. Think of 'de-' as pulling something apart from its envelope.
From Old French voloper/envelopper (to wrap). The same root appears in 'envelope.' So développer literally means to unwrap or unfold.
A phonetic extension of the root, part of the verb stem développer. Not a separate meaningful morpheme — just part of how the verb is built.

words.exampleSentence: Le développement de cette région a été rapide ces dernières années.
words.exampleTranslation: The development of this region has been rapid in recent years.
words.flag: 5+ parts — velop/pe boundary is phonetic rather than clean morpheme split

parts.homeLangDetails: Here dé- reverses or undoes the idea of setting something in place — to de-termine is to un-fix or undo a boundary. Combined with pré-, the whole word means to fix something in advance.

words.translationLong: to predetermine, to decide in advance
words.exampleSentence: Certaines théories affirment que le destin est prédéterminé.
words.exampleTranslation: Some theories claim that fate is predetermined.
words.flag: note: dé- + termin- could arguably be one root 'déterminer'; split reflects morpheme transparency

words.exampleSentence: Le changement était si imperceptible qu'on ne le remarquait pas.
words.exampleTranslation: The change was so imperceptible that no one noticed it.

parts.homeLangDetails: From Latin seligere (to pick out, to select). The sé- here is a reduced form of se- (apart), part of the root, not a freestanding prefix — it fused into 'sélection' long ago.

words.exampleSentence: La présélection des candidats a été effectuée par un jury indépendant.
words.exampleTranslation: The pre-selection of candidates was carried out by an independent jury.

words.exampleSentence: L'immobilisation du blessé était nécessaire avant le transport.
words.exampleTranslation: Immobilizing the injured person was necessary before transport.

parts.homeLangDetails: Here dé- reverses or undoes the action — to de-responsibilize someone is to strip them of their sense of responsibility.
From Latin responsabilis — the stem that also gives us 'responsible' and 'responsibility'. The -abil- part corresponds to the English '-ible/-able' pattern.
Part of the -iser suffix that turns a noun or adjective into a verb, equivalent to English '-ize'. Together with the infinitive ending -er it forms the full suffix -iser.

words.translationLong: to relieve of responsibility, to make someone feel unaccountable
words.exampleSentence: Ce système risque de déresponsabiliser les employés.
words.exampleTranslation: This system risks making employees feel unaccountable.
words.flag: 5 parts — word is a layered derivation: dé + responsabil(ité) + is(er) + er
only 1 part — this appears to be a fragment or typo, not a standalone French word

parts.homeLangDetails: This does not appear to be a standalone French word. It may be a fragment of a word ending in -itement, such as poliment, or a typo. No morpheme analysis can be confidently provided.
From croire (to believe). The spelling shifts from croir- to croy- before the suffix — same root you see in croyance (belief) and incroyable (unbelievable).

Means something can be done, exactly like English "-able/-ible". Mangeable (edible, literally eatable), compréhensible (comprehensible), acceptable (acceptable). Most of these map directly onto English equivalents, making them easy to guess.

words.exampleSentence: Son histoire est à peine croyable.
words.flag: only 2 parts, but note: 'croyable' is mostly used in negated form 'incroyable'
